experiment: Native MCP vs OpenLakehouse Agent Coupling
research_question: How does agent integration complexity compare between native platform connectors and the OpenLakehouse Canonical Lakehouse Model (CLM) when an agent must access data across multiple lakehouse platforms?

approaches.description: Agent connects directly to Databricks MCP server or SQL connector
approaches.agent_code_changes_when_adding_platform: Full rewrite or parallel integration required
approaches.response_contract: Databricks-specific (Unity Catalog REST + SQL Warehouse schema)

approaches.description: Agent connects directly to AWS Glue/Athena APIs or AWS MCP server
approaches.agent_code_changes_when_adding_platform: Full rewrite or parallel integration required

approaches.description: Agent connects to both Databricks and AWS MCP endpoints separately
approaches.agent_code_changes_when_adding_platform: New endpoint config + parser + branches per operation
approaches.response_contract: Mixed — agent branches on platform at every step
approaches.vendor_lock_in: High (multiplied; grows linearly with platform count)

approaches.description: Agent connects to OpenLakehouse; receives Canonical Lakehouse Model responses
approaches.agent_code_changes_when_adding_platform: Zero — adapter registered server-side; agent unchanged
approaches.vendor_lock_in: Reduced — agent decoupled from platform-specific schemas via CLM abstraction

tool_comparison.Databricks Native: No — agent tied to Databricks API schema
tool_comparison.AWS Native: No — agent tied to AWS API schema
tool_comparison.OpenLakehouse CLM: Yes — CLM defines stable canonical fields independent of platform

agent_portability_metrics.agent_code_changes_when_adding_platform: Zero — adapter registered server-side; agent unchanged

zero_agent_modification_property.description: Adding a new platform adapter to OpenLakehouse requires zero changes to agent code, MCP tool names, or canonical response parsers. Verified in Experiment 5.
